Northern Province, Fifth Empire
Outer Eastern Islands, 5 miles north-east of Eastern City

In the early pale grey of sunrise in the outer Eastern Islands, the fisherman Gojo Gomani waded out to survey the damage done to his reed-boat.
There had been another strange storm in the night. Gojo understood storms well, having lived nearly four decades as a fisherman and two as a fisherman’s son. But he had never seen lightning that shot up from the ocean towards the sky, rain that fell without even single cloud in sight, or giant waves that suddenly erupted out of calm waters to overturn the fishing boats and spit them up onto the shore. 
It reeked of mage-work, but not of the usual Elemental kind that was practiced in the Northern provinces. Elemental mages could easily call up an ocean storm, but they were as bound to the laws of nature as they were to the laws of the government. They could no more bring forth lightning from the sea as they could create a storm without the Nobility knowing about it. And Gojo had heard soon enough that the Nobility knew nothing about it, for he sold fresh oysters in Eastern City on market days, and his oyster cart sat squarely at the Three Crossroads. One road led to the Lords’ Court, one travelled south to the border of the Southern provinces, one wound north-west to the Forgotten Mountains, and all three of them were buzzing with news: it was not the work of any registered mage, and the Nobility were as at a loss as everyone else. Perhaps, it was speculated, it was the work of mages in the south, where Elemental magic was not the only kind. Rumours quickly circulated about a possible attack, and the breaking of the Alliance between the provinces; but these were laid to rest when a formal investigation was done. The Southern Nobility had heard nothing of the storms.
It had been a week now since the last one, and Gojo had been hopeful. He’d left his reed-boat, repaired several times now, anchored for the past three nights and there had been nothing. But at midnight the rain falling on his roof had awoken him, and when he’d run outside, hoping it was a normal spring thunderstorm, he saw rain pouring out of a cloudless sky. Gojo had thought to rescue his boat, but he knew by the sound of the crashing waves that it was already too late, and so he waited till morning.
He was still thirty paces from the overturned boat when he heard something. 
The fisherman’s shell amulets tied to the brim of his hat abruptly fell silent as he froze, knee-deep in the water. The sound had echoed faintly from across the water, and he squinted into the rising sun. There was nothing there. 
Then he heard it again, and he held his breath, straining for the noise. It sounded like singing. But the horizon was empty, save a flock of seagulls floating out a little ways from the boat, and seagulls did not sing. 
And then a strange movement suddenly became apparent to him in the air. A shape, or shapes, that blended in almost perfectly with the pale blue sky, but glimmered slightly around the edges. 
There were perhaps a dozen of them, flying directly toward him, and the singing grew louder. Gojo could tell it was human voices, for he could faintly hear the baritones of men holding a low note, the higher treble of women, and a thin clear wave of burbling notes that could only be children. 
What were these creatures? They were not flying like any birds he had seen before. He could make out something akin to wings from this distance, but instead of flapping, they seemed to undulate in waves, like ripples gently rolling outwards from a thrown pebble. It was like seaweed flowing in the reef tide, a distinctly ocean movement, not an aerial movement.
It occurred to Gojo to run. If he turned now, he could leave the beach before the creatures saw him. It would only take him five minutes to reach the village. If the visitors were hostile, at least the mages would be prepared. 
But with age came a surprising amount of fearlessness, and Gojo found he was far more curious than he was afraid. He stood staring at the approaching creatures, and suddenly his creased face momentarily smoothed out in surprise as he recognized what they were.
They were squid. Giant flying squid, almost perfectly camouflaged to match the hue of the sky. What he had thought to be wings were the creatures’ fins, vibrating phosphorescent ribbons of blue and gold. They were massive beasts, easily the size of small whales. They seemed effortless in flight, practically motionless, except for the shimmering fins rippling outwards. Gojo was close enough to see that their bellies were changing colour from the pale blue of the sky to match the more mottled green of the shallow waters. They were flying downwards. They were coming to land.
The singing grew louder as the squid approached, and Gojo realized that there must be people riding atop their backs. Perhaps a hundred or more in all. A full choir. The animals descended upon the surface of the water, and he saw he was right. There were a dozen people clustered down the middle of each animal. Some of them seemed to be holding onto ropes, but others were simply balancing. A few were sitting on the mottled, ever-changing skin.
The ripple from their landing reached Gojo a few seconds later, and then passed him to spread upon the shore. The noise of the wave was the only sound on the beach. The singing had stopped, but Gojo hadn’t noticed. His eyes were locked on the squid, floating a mere twenty meters from where he stood. One of the creatures had left the line of the others, and was swimming forward to meet him.
A man was walking directly down the spine of the animal. He was dressed in greenish-gray, in a sleeveless garment that revealed sun-darkened arms with strange circular marks upon them, marks that ran up from his wrists to his neck, and across his forehead. He reached the tip of the squid, and stood like a strange emperor, the squid’s fins flowing like a royal carpet of iridescent lights from either side of his bare feet. 
The creature stopped ten metres from the fisherman. The water was too shallow for it to come any further, and it floated half-submerged, a strange semi-translucent island with flickering tentacles that swished lazily around in the shallows like eels. It paid no attention at all to Gojo, or to the man who stood at its nose to greet him.
The man raised his arms, open-palmed, to show he carried no weapons. He had a graying beard, and behind it, a kindly, weathered face. Gojo could see that the man was well beyond his middle years, despite his powerful build and broad stature. He held his own arms up slowly, a reflection of the greeting, and called:
“I am <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>, a fisherman of these islands. Who are you, and what brings you here?”
The rider smiled, and shouted back: “We are called the Kikran, and we do not come with ill intent. We are travellers, and the storms brought us here, fisherman.” 
“The storms!” Gojo exclaimed. “Are you mages? Is this weather of your doing?”
The man shook his head. “We have no mages among us. We are nomads, not magic men. We no more created this storm than we meant to land here! Our beasts are old and dying and grow weary of long distances. We had meant to reach their breeding grounds by now.”
“Where do those lie?” Gojo asked. He was surprised when the man shrugged. 
“It changes. Our creatures follow an ancient of their kind, a mother, or a queen, if you will. Only she does not fly, but wanders the depths of the ocean. We go where she goes, and when it is time, she leads the squid to a new breeding ground.” He frowned. “We were following her path. But early last week, we lost the trail. It seems she has disappeared.”
Gojo blinked. “Disappeared? From the storm, perhaps?”
“Perhaps. I have never seen a storm like this,” the man said. “But she vanished before we reached it – probably why we flew into it at all.” He sighed. “And now we are stranded. Temporarily at least. Will you allow us refuge on these islands? What are they called, and to whom do they belong?”
Gojo opened his mouth to answer, but then hesitated. 
The man could be lying, he realized. He had nothing to go by, no knowledge of these people or their powers. But he did not know what else could be done, if they were indeed stranded. They appeared to be unarmed, and they had been friendly enough.
Gojo glanced at the Kikran standing behind the man and took in the unmistakable weariness on their faces and their storm-torn clothing. Someone had to make a decision, and there was no one else to do it, so he said:
“These are the outer Eastern Islands of the Northern provinces. They belong to the governing Lords, and are inhabited mostly by fishermen such as myself. My village is just up from the beach. There you will be welcome to rest.”

Chapter One – The Kikran’s Arrival

Gojo had lived long enough by the sea to know when a simple offer could lead to a tide of change. As the Kikran leader nodded his thanks and turned back to his colossal companion, Gojo watched the squadron of skyborne squids aligning offshore. They were creatures of wonders, adorned with the spectrum of the heavens upon their backs.

The settled villagers stirred awake with the rising sun, oblivious to the arrival that would soon interrupt their calm seascape life. Gojo began the trek back to shore, his gaze sweeping back frequently to the Kikran who were now carefully guiding their mounts closer to land.

Upon reaching his overturned reed-boat, the fisherman heaved a sigh. It was, as he expected, damaged beyond repair. He had known its contours like the lines of his own weathered hands, each repair a story, each break a lesson in resilience. Now it was little more than driftwood, and Gojo could not suppress the twinge of loss that echoed deep within him.

He saw the shell amulets he wore—a gift from his late wife, humming softly with the ocean breeze, reminding him that some elements in life remain, even as others are cast adrift. Shaking off melancholy, Gojo made his way to the village, feeling the weight of responsibility settling upon his shoulders like a fisherman's net heavy with the day’s catch.

Villagers emerged from their homes, faces etched with the same curious confusion that had first crossed Gojo's face upon seeing the Kikran. He motioned to them, words preemptively answering the rush of questions.

"Strangers from afar," he briefly explained. "Borne on storm and sea, the Kikran come seeking refuge, their visit as unexpected to them as to us."

A murmur rippled through the crowd, where fear and intrigue were interwoven as tightly as the nets they cast each day—a community defined by simplicity, now touched by the extraordinary. Gojo led the villagers to the beach, making the introductions that would bridge the worlds of the fishermen and the nomads from the sky.

The Kikran disembarked from their aquatic steeds, each one a spectacle of raw wonderment for the island folk, who could scarce imagine such colossal beasts wielding the power of flight. In turn, the Kikran gazed in fascination upon the quaint village—so far removed from their usual horizons, so densely woven with the unity of family and neighbor.

Resilient and free of pretense, the fishermen opened their homes and hearts to the Kikran, sharing provisions and stories by firesides that now held a glow of shared humanity. The nomads spoke of skies and empires beyond the fisherman’s simple imagination, of squids nurtured in deep ocean trenches and trained for the skies. In return, the fishermen shared tales of azure waters, of the bounty of the sea, and of the storms that seemed to rise from the very wrath of gods.

It was during these exchanges, over shared meals of smoked fish and cracked oysters, that Gojo heard a word unfamiliar and yet resounding with a depth that sent a jolt through him.

"Aethermancy," explained a Kikran elder, his voice like gravel washed ashore. "The art that flows not just within the elements we see but draws on the essence that is beyond—the Aether. It is the magic of the universe's fabric itself."

Aethermancy was neither Water nor Fire, Earth nor Air, but something more, a binding force. It was, the elder elaborated, forbidden knowledge in many lands, for its wielders often found themselves seduced by the echo of creation it possessed. It was the music to which the world danced, largely unheard by mortal ears.

In the village square, as the days passed into the gentle grip of evenings, Gojo noticed a change in the atmosphere—a sense of unity. The islanders and Kikran, once strangers, now laughed together, unfettered by the distance of their origins. This harmony, born from unexpected tumult, reflected something larger—a world capable of enduring, of evolving beyond the realms of storms.

Gojo Gomani, fisherman of the Outer Eastern Islands, stood by the water's edge, shell amulets clinking softly, and gazed out at the benign squids, now at peace in the shallow bay. He realized that the horizon will always hold unknowns, but the beauty of existence lay in the meeting of tides. Whether spurred by mage-work or woven by the unheard song of Aether, life was an ocean of stories intertwined, boundless and deep, and he was now part of one greater than he'd ever known.

In his heart, a peace settled that he had not felt since his youthful days—since before he knew the burden of a storm's chaos. For the world, he understood, was vaster than the sea, as mysterious as the Kikran's origins, and as wondrous as the tales yet to be told.

Chapter Two – The Echoes of Aether

As the days unfolded, the Kikran and the villagers of the Outer Eastern Islands became interlaced in ways neither could have predicted. The Kikran's knowledge of the skies and of Aethermancy mesmerized the fisherfolk, who had only known the sea's surface and the myths their ancestors whispered about the stars.

One evening, as fuchsia twilight draped over the horizon, the Kikran leader, a man known as Bozan, invited Gojo to observe a tradition seldom shared with outsiders. Beneath the waning crescent of the early moon, they embarked upon a ritual that harkened to the heart of Aether.

"We Kikran commune with the Aether to understand our path," Bozan explained, his eyes shining with a reverence for forces beyond mortal sight. "It guides our migrations, our squids in flight, and our lives along the invisible streams of the world."

Around a carefully drawn circle of glimmering dust that Bozan claimed was gathered from the wake of shooting stars, the Kikran began their ancient chants. A quiver ran through the air as the multitude of voices rose together, harmonizing with a melody that seemed to pull at the very threads of reality.

Gojo, anchored beside Bozan outside the circle, watched as the Aethermancers channeled an energy unseen but deeply felt. The air within the circle shimmered with translucent hues, each tone a symphony of otherworldly resonance. Gojo's breath hitched as he sensed a connection—a glimpse into a world that typically lay shrouded from mortal perception.

The ritual unveiled a vision that wove into existence before them: a graceful, undulating path, rippling through the cosmos. It was the trail of a great Celestial Squid, the mother Bozan had spoken of—their guide across vast distances, their compass in an endless sky.

"The Aether speaks," Bozan murmured. "Our mother still lives, yet her call is faint... troubled."

It was then that Gojo spoke hesitantly of the strange storms, of his suspicion of Aethermancy at play, uncontrolled and wild—an unmistakable disturbance in the world’s elements.

Bozan's gaze snapped to Gojo, a sudden intensity burning within. "You believe these storms are not natural... that they are woven from the Aether itself?"

Gojo nodded, uncertainty furrowing his brow. "Is it not possible? The storms came without warning, without reason. They reek of power, untamed and masterless."

The ritual ceased, the vision dissipating like mist at sunrise, leaving behind more questions than answers. Bozan stepped closer to Gojo, placing a firm hand upon his shoulder.

"We will seek this storm's source, Gojo Gomani," Bozan promised, his determination steely as the ocean's depths. "If Aethermancy is indeed at play, estranging us from our guide, then we must act. For the balance of Aether is sacred—it is the unity of all that is, was, and will be."

Preparations began at dawn. A convergence of Kikran expertise and the sailors’ knowledge shaped their plan. Small boats were reinforced, supplies were gathered, and villagers freely offered their aid. A trust had been forged, no longer as strangers, but as kindred spirits united by a shared peril.

As they set out to confront the heart of the maelstrom, Gojo stood at the helm of the leading vessel, his gaze set seaward with unwavering resolve. The Aether's whispers called to him, imbuing the fisherman with a purpose that transcended his island's bounds.

Bozan stood alongside him, his circular marks—a sigil of his accord with the Aether—glimmering faintly beneath the sun.Their path lay uncertain before them, into the domain of tempests and uncharted waters.

By Bozan's mystic sight and Gojo's seasoned instincts, they navigated the swells, riding the edge of the storm that had ensnared their fate. The seas roared, but so did the heart of the fisherman who had once thought his tales had reached their end. Now, as the Aether's silent thunder resonated within his chest, Gojo Gomani knew his story—it was only just beginning.

Chapter Three – Into the Maelstrom

The waters churned beneath their vessel, a reflection of the tumult in the skies above. Gojo gripped the rudder with firm hands, each muscle earned through a lifetime of wrestling with the sea. Beside him, Bozan stood solemn, his eyes scanning the horizon for a sign, a ripple in the pattern that might lead them to the Aetheric disturbance.

The fleet of boats, an odd assembly of Kikran squid-creatures and sturdy fisherman's skiffs, cut through the waves, bound together by an ancient, spiritual urgency. Their goal was as much a mystery as their path—a quest formed of necessity and trust, seeking a storm's heart and braving Aether's unknown depths.

As the squids' undulations mirrored the motions of the sea, the fisherfolk murmured prayers to old gods and older currents. Their children, raised to respect the ocean's might, looked on with wide eyes, bearing witness to the unfolding legend.

Whispers of Bozan's tales had woven through the village like the morning mist. Tales of Aether's streams, flowing invisible and interconnecting all life, tales that had long ago blended into their collective mythology. Now, that myth grappled with reality, the lines between fable and fact as blurred as the sea blending into the stormy sky.

The further they traveled, the thicker the mist became; it swirled around them, a dance of sea spray and Aether's breath. Then, through the veil, a staggering sight loomed—a vortex on the ocean’s surface, a swirling gateway that beckoned and repelled with equal force.

"This is it," Bozan declared, his voice carrying over the tempest's roar. "The disruption in Aether. This maelstrom is unnatural, born of a rift, a wound in the world's fabric. To close it, we must understand it—meld with its turmoil and calm its fury."

The fleet aligned in a delicate formation, each vessel positioning according to a pattern dictated by Aether’s subtle flows. Bozan began to chant, a call to the universe's energies in a language that rumbled like the deep sea. The Kikran joined in chorus, their voices an anchored foundation against the chaos.

Gojo, free of enchantments but rich in understanding, directed the boats with navigational commands that found harmony with the Kikran's incantations. The storm responded, its winds howling in counterpoint, and the maelstrom pulsed as if alive—with wrath, with pain.

A surge of Aetheric energy burst forth from Bozan, winding through the air with tendrils of radiant blue light. It touched each squid and each boat, forming a luminescent web upon the waters. Gojo felt the surge, a shock of power that resonated in his bones, and for a moment, he feared they would be swept into the abyss.

But the ritual held. The maelstrom's rage stilled as the Aetheric network enfolded it, a balm for turmoil and a beacon for guidance. Within the eye of the storm, reality wavered, revealing glimpses of other worlds, other streams—paths of Aether that connected and cradled existence.

In that timeless center, the Celestial Squid emerged like a phantom of the deep, its colossal form outlined in starlight. Its path had been torn asunder, its children led astray by the rend in Aether it so vigilantly followed.

The revelation blanketed the fleet in awe. A hush fell, as all witnessed the celestial entity glide forward, its form gliding past the squid-mounts with a touch that whispered of home. The mother of the Kikran had sought her brood across the cosmos, and now, found them united with the obstinate souls who faced the storm with valor.

Commands were unnecessary. The squids moved instinctively, aligning with their matriarch, her form guiding them to close the maelstrom's malicious swirl. With each passing second, the whirlpool diminished, the fracture mending as Aether resumed its rightful course.

As the storm calmed to a gentle murmur, Gojo released his hold on the rudder. His gaze met Bozan's, a moment of understanding unsullied by language—a mutual recognition that they had witnessed a splendor beyond comprehension.

As the fleet made their way back to the village, riding on tranquil seas under a sky of newfound clarity, they were not just fishermen and nomads, but pioneers who had ventured into the unknown and returned with a shared secret—a twilight tale of squids and storms, of wonder and unity.

The Outer Eastern Islands would no longer be a mere footnote on the maps of empires and cartographers. The villagers would forever speak of the day when Aether called upon their courage, and their simple lives intersected with the infinite.

Gojo, once a fisherman resigned to end his days amid nets and shells, now found himself christened anew—an Aether-navigator, a title unclaimed but wholly his. And when the oysters once again filled his cart at the Three Crossroads, a new story would pass from his lips—a testament to the day the sea sang with stars and the Kikran graced the skies above.

For in the aftermath of storms, lay new dawns, and every dawn, the promise of tales yet to weave into the tapestry of the Aether's endless saga.

Chapter Four – The Aether's Song

The return to the village was a parade of quiet triumph; a caravan adorned not with spoils of war, but harbingers of harmony. Children ran alongside the smoothed squids, their laughter knitting into the evening air. Elders watched from stoops and doorways, their skepticism waning in the wake of wonder.

And in the evening that followed, a great feast unfurled beneath a tapestry of stars – the cosmos, once a backdrop to the villagers' tales, had unveiled its intimacy to them. The Kikran, once enigmatic wanderers of the sky, now broke bread with the fisherfolk, their stories a shared currency of camaraderie.

Gojo, seated at the table's head beside Bozan and the village matriarch, felt the eyes of the community upon him. The oyster shells that dangled from his hat caught the firelight, casting prismatic fragments across his leathery, sea-brushed face. He lifted his gaze to meet theirs not with the solemnity of a patriarch but with the mischievous twinkle of a lorekeeper on the cusp of a tale.

"This night," Gojo began, his voice carrying over the crackle of the fire, "we have nudged the course of legends. We spoke with Aether as one does with an old friend, and it heeded our call. But let us not forget the sea's lessons, for she is a grand mistress of the unexpected."

A nod from Bozan, the quiet intensity of his presence a grounding force. "Aether binds us, like threads of a net cast wide across the heavens," he agreed, his words a pledge to the unseen fabric that wrapped around and within all. "Our journey continues, but for a moment, we bask in the glow of nature's dance, a step learned, a step shared."

The villagers leaned in, pulling wisdom from their words, their faces lantern-lit and expectant. The Kikran squids, visible from the banquet on the shore, pulsed with a faint rhythm—like the heartbeat of the world, audible to those who had felt its echoes in the maelstrom.

And in that moment, as Gojo raised his cup in salute, the fisherman felt a kinship more profound than any storm could unsettle. He concluded, "To the Aether's song, may it ever find us eager to dance."

The night lengthened, the fire's warmth radiated, and the stars shone with the brilliance of shared secrets. As elders traded wisdom with Kikran nomads and children practiced the art of squid-greeting, the fishermen of the Outer Eastern Islands rewove the fabric of their lives with newfound threads, shimmering with possibility.

The squids, with their celestial guide returned, would remain just long enough for the villagers to grow accustomed to their iridescent presence. When the time came for the Kikran to depart, seeking new currents in the sky, they would leave behind more than memories; they would leave behind a legacy emboldened by an Aetheric embrace.

For the villagers, each sunrise brought tales of the sea and now, of the sky as well. And as Gojo looked towards the horizon each dawn, his thoughts adrift on the tide, he knew the storms had been but a prelude to a symphony—the notes of which would resonate through generation and aeon, a crescendo of the universe's unending melody.

In the Outer Eastern Islands, a fisherman's tale had become a saga entwined with the cosmos. And in his heart, Gojo carried the Aether's song, a melody of storms and stars, woven into the net of his life—each thread a story, each knot a beginning.
